title: Černý conjecture (synchronizing automata)
statement: Every synchronizing DFA on n states admits a synchronizing (reset) word of length at most (n−1)². The best known general upper bound is cubic in n; the quadratic bound is open.

unknowns.description: a proof of the (n−1)² reset-word bound (or a family beating it)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of the quadratic bound
exhibit an n-state synchronizing DFA whose shortest reset word exceeds (n−1)²

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: best known upper bound ~ (n³−n)/6 (Pin/Frankl; Szykuła 2018 improved the constant)

title: Missing Moore graph (degree 57, diameter 2)
statement: Does a Moore graph of degree 57 and diameter 2 exist — a 57-regular graph on 3250 vertices with girth 5? By Hoffman–Singleton such graphs exist only for degree 2, 3, 7, 57, and the degree-57 case is the sole one whose existence is undecided.

unknowns.description: a 57-regular girth-5 graph on 3250 vertices, or a proof none exists

criteria.test: exhibit the graph (a valid construction)
an accepted non-existence proof

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: known: any such graph has a trivial automorphism group (Mačaj–Širáň 2010)

title: Rectangle-free grid coloring
statement: For which grid sizes m×n does a c-coloring exist with no monochromatic combinatorial rectangle (four cells at the intersections of two rows and two columns all sharing one color)? Determine, for each number of colors c, the largest grid that is c-colorable rectangle-free.

unknowns.description: the exact rectangle-free threshold grid for each color count c (a coloring or an impossibility bound)

criteria.test: matching bounds pinning the largest c-colorable grid
exhibit a rectangle-free c-coloring of a grid conjectured to be uncolorable

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: the 4-coloring of the 17×17 grid was resolved (Steinbach–Posthoff 2012)

title: Combinatorial polynomial Hirsch conjecture
statement: In the purely combinatorial abstraction of polytope vertex–facet adjacency, is the diameter of the "graph" bounded by a polynomial in the number of facets and the dimension? A polynomial combinatorial bound would give a polynomial bound on polytope diameters (a weak polynomial Hirsch conjecture).

unknowns.description: a polynomial upper bound on the combinatorial diameter (or a super-polynomial lower bound)

criteria.test: an accepted polynomial upper bound on the combinatorial diameter

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: connected to the (disproved exact) Hirsch conjecture — Santos 2012 counterexample

title: Computable counterexample to Sarnak's Möbius conjecture
statement: Is there a boolean function computable within a weak complexity class (e.g. AC⁰, or small circuits) that correlates non-trivially with the Möbius function μ(n)? Sarnak's conjecture predicts μ is disjoint from all low-complexity/deterministic sequences; a computable correlation would refute it in that model.

unknowns.description: an efficiently-computable sequence correlating with μ, or a proof of Möbius disjointness for the class

criteria.test: an accepted proof of Möbius disjointness for the complexity class
exhibit a low-complexity function correlating with μ (a counterexample)

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: related to the Chowla conjecture; known unconditionally for AC⁰ (Green 2012)

title: 3-sphere recognition: exact complexity
statement: Determine the exact computational complexity of recognizing the 3-sphere from a triangulation. It is known to lie in NP (Schleimer) and, assuming GRH, also in coNP (Hass–Kuperberg); whether it is NP-complete, polynomial-time, or of intermediate complexity is open.

unknowns.description: a tight complexity classification of 3-sphere recognition

criteria.test: an accepted complexity classification (e.g. a polynomial algorithm or an NP-hardness proof)

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: in NP: Schleimer 2004; in coNP under GRH: Hass–Kuperberg

title: Approximating the sign rank of a matrix
statement: What is the complexity of approximating the sign rank (dimension complexity) of a ±1 matrix — the least dimension in which the sign pattern is realizable by a real arrangement? Even coarse approximation is not known to be tractable, and exact computation is hard.

unknowns.description: an approximation algorithm for sign rank, or a hardness-of-approximation result

criteria.test: an accepted approximation guarantee or matching hardness result

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: sign rank / dimension complexity in communication complexity
exact sign rank decision is ∃ℝ-complete (Basri et al.; Bhangale–Kopparty 2015)

title: Optimal algorithm for the girth of a sparse graph
statement: Is there an algorithm computing the girth (shortest cycle length) of a sparse graph substantially faster than the best known bounds — ideally near-linear time, or a fine-grained lower bound explaining the barrier? The exact optimal running time is open.

unknowns.description: a faster girth algorithm or a matching (fine-grained) lower bound

criteria.test: an accepted algorithm meeting a matching lower bound

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: girth vs. APSP / triangle-detection fine-grained barriers

title: Frankl's union-closed sets conjecture
statement: In every finite union-closed family of sets (closed under pairwise unions, not just {∅}), some element belongs to at least half of the sets. Open in general; an information-theoretic argument gives a constant of ≈ 0.38 rather than 1/2.

unknowns.description: a proof of the ≥ n/2 frequency bound (or a counterexample family)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of the half-frequency bound
exhibit a union-closed family with every element in < half the sets

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: PARTIAL: Gilmer 2022 (arXiv:2211.09055) proved a constant ≥ 0.01, sharpened to (3−√5)/2 ≈ 0.38 by Sawin, Chase–Lovett, Alweiss–Huang–Sellke

title: Intersecting families of triangulations (EKR-type)
statement: Among the triangulations of a convex n-gon, call two "intersecting" if they share a common diagonal. Conjecturally the largest pairwise-intersecting family has the size of a maximal "star" — all triangulations through one fixed diagonal — an Erdős–Ko–Rado phenomenon for the associahedron.

unknowns.description: a proof that a maximum intersecting family of triangulations is a star (or a larger family)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of the star-maximality bound

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: Olarte–Santos et al. / an EKR-analogue on the associahedron

title: Komlós vector-balancing conjecture
statement: There is an absolute constant K such that for any vectors v₁,…,vₙ ∈ ℝⁿ with ‖vᵢ‖₂ ≤ 1 one can choose signs εᵢ ∈ {±1} with ‖Σ εᵢ vᵢ‖_∞ ≤ K, independent of n. The best known bound (Banaszczyk) is O(√(log n)); a universal constant is open.

unknowns.description: a sign choice achieving an O(1) discrepancy bound (or a proof that none can)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of the constant-K bound
exhibit vectors forcing unbounded signed ℓ∞ discrepancy

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: Komlós conjecture; implies the Beck–Fiala conjecture

title: Amenability of Thompson's group F
statement: Is Richard Thompson's group F amenable? F is a finitely presented group of piecewise-linear homeomorphisms of [0,1]; whether it admits an invariant mean has resisted repeated proof attempts in both directions.

unknowns.description: a proof that F is amenable, or a proof that it is not

criteria.test: an accepted proof settling amenability of F

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: many announced proofs (both ways) later withdrawn

title: Complex structure on the 6-sphere
statement: Does S⁶ admit an integrable almost-complex (i.e. a genuine complex) structure? S⁶ carries an almost-complex structure from the octonions, but whether an integrable one exists — making S⁶ a complex manifold — is a long-standing open problem.

unknowns.description: a construction of an integrable complex structure on S⁶, or a proof of non-existence

criteria.test: an accepted proof (construction or obstruction)

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: several announced proofs (incl. Atiyah, Chern) not accepted

title: Common fixed point of two commuting maps of the disk
statement: Do every two commuting continuous maps of the closed 2-disk into itself have a common fixed point? Each has a fixed point by Brouwer; whether commuting pairs always share one is open (the analogous statement fails for the interval only in higher-pathology settings).

unknowns.description: a proof of a common fixed point (or a commuting pair with none)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of a common fixed point
exhibit commuting continuous self-maps of the disk with no common fixed point

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: Boyce & Huneke 1969 disproved the interval version

title: Polynomial bijection ℚ×ℚ → ℚ
statement: Is there a polynomial f ∈ ℚ[x,y] that is a bijection from ℚ×ℚ onto ℚ? Such "pairing polynomials" exist over ℕ (Cantor), but whether one exists over the rationals is open; a conditional non-existence follows from a case of the Bombieri–Lang conjecture.

unknowns.description: a rational polynomial bijection ℚ² → ℚ, or a proof none exists

criteria.test: exhibit and verify a polynomial bijection
an accepted non-existence proof

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

title: If 2^x and 3^x are integers, is x?
statement: If both 2^x and 3^x are integers for a real x, must x itself be an integer? A special case of the four-exponentials conjecture; open despite its elementary statement.

unknowns.description: a proof that x must be an integer (or a real counterexample)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of the integrality of x

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: special case of the four-exponentials conjecture

title: A square with only digits 0 and 1
statement: Is there an integer N, other than a power of 10, whose square N² is written in base 10 using only the digits 0 and 1? No such N is known below very large search bounds, and its existence or impossibility is open.

unknowns.description: such an N, or a proof that only powers of 10 qualify

criteria.test: an accepted proof that no non-10^k square has only 0/1 digits
exhibit N ≠ 10^k with N² using only digits 0 and 1

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

title: Volumes of constant-width bodies in high dimensions
statement: Do the minimal-volume convex bodies of constant width 1 in ℝⁿ have volume exponentially smaller than the unit-width ball? Schramm proved a lower bound of (√3 − 1)ⁿ times the ball; whether the true minimum is exponentially small (as conjectured) is open.

unknowns.description: the asymptotic minimal volume of constant-width bodies (matching bounds)

criteria.test: matching asymptotic bounds pinning the minimal volume

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: Kalai–Oliveros–Bezdek surveys; recent progress bounding the ratio < 1 (2024)

title: Covering the unit square by 1/k × 1/(k+1) rectangles
statement: The rectangles of size 1/k × 1/(k+1) for k = 1,2,3,… have areas summing to exactly 1. Can they be packed without overlap to tile (cover) the unit square exactly? Existence of such a perfect packing is open (the Meir–Moser problem).

unknowns.description: a perfect packing of the 1/k×1/(k+1) rectangles into the unit square, or a proof none exists

criteria.test: exhibit a valid non-overlapping perfect packing
an accepted impossibility proof

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: known: they pack into a square of area 1 + ε for every ε > 0 (Paulhus 1998)

title: Nontrivial acyclic finite group
statement: Is there a nontrivial finite group whose reduced integral group homology vanishes in all positive degrees (an acyclic finite group)? Superperfect finite groups exist (e.g. binary icosahedral), but full acyclicity of a nontrivial finite group is not known to occur or to be impossible.

unknowns.description: such a group, or a proof that none exists

criteria.test: an accepted example or a non-existence proof

provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the source; body text not copied (CC BY-SA). The backlink is the attribution.

refs: related to superperfect groups and the Schur multiplier
